attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a four day history of pale stools, dark urine and itching. He does not have any other symptoms. His medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed four years ago which is currently being treated with tamsulosin. He underwent a gastric bypass surgery for morbid obesity 10 years ago. The physical examination is unremarkable apart from yellow sclera. Liver function tests are arranged (Image 1). A CT scan show a dilated common bile duct, but no obvious underlying cause.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical picture suggests obstructive jaundice and this is confirmed on the liver function tests. There are a number of possible causes and the next step is to confirm the obstruction and to identify its location, extent and likely cause. Whilst a CT can most usually identify the site and cause - particularly if it is a mass lesion - in this case it has not provided that detail. The two most likely causes of obstructive jaundice to consider will be a carcinoma of the head of the pancreas or common duct stones. Even in the absence of a mass lesion, it would be prudent to do tumour marker studies in addition to imaging. However, the focus in this case is the determination of the most appropriate imaging investigation.

In most instances ERCP would the preferred diagnostic investigation, either obtaining a tissue diagnosis and/or overcoming the obstruction with insertion of biliary stent. However due to the patient's gastric bypass history, ERCP is not going to be feasible in this case. Gastric bypass is a relative contraindication of ERCP, due to the anatomical variation leading to difficulty in accessing the duodenal limb (Roux-en-Y bypass). One possible approach might be laparoscopic cannulation of the distal gastric pouch and gaining access to the common bile duct by this route. However, a most practical solution is an MRCP. This is a non-invasive procedure and will provide an accurate picture of the biliary tree. It will help the clinician determine the cause and best approach to the problem.

Endoscopic ultrasound can provide useful information about the likely cause of biliary obstruction and facilitate the biopsy of a mass lesion, but in this case with the altered upper digestive tract anatomy it is not going to be feasible.

There is no indication for a diagnostic laparoscopy in this case, but could be considered as a means of obtaining a tissue diagnosis if there was more evidence that the underlying problem might be a pancreatic cancer. It is more commonly done in patients with stomach carcinoma looking for peritoneal metastases. Moreover in a 65-year-old patient with significant co-morbidities such as this one, non-invasive measures will be preferred.

Abdominal ultrasound is unlikely to give additional information that the CT-scan has not already provide.
learningPoints: When the anatomy facilitates it, ERCP is the most preferred diagnostic tool for pancreatic cancer.
Gastric bypass surgery will prevent regular endoscopic access to the biliary tree
references: Hanada K, Minami T, Shimizu A, Fukuhara M, Yano S, Sasaki K, Koda M, Sugiyama K, Yonehara S, Yanagisawa A. Roles of ERCP in the early diagnosis of pancreatic cancer. Diagnostics. 2019 Mar 7;9(1):30.
Mudgal P, Morgan, M. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP). Radiopaedia.2022 Accessed on 07 Sep 2022. Available via: https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-32557
Knipe H, Worsley, C. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. Radiopaedia. 2022. Accessed on 07 Sep 2022. Available via: https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-35490
